sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=0, col=38), MOUSE(row=1, col=32).
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 132, level 1.

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: c_bar=35, P=29. All previous hypotheses failed. Need to re-examine the bar's location and structure.
- Plan: Check ascii to verify bar position. Try clicking on the bar at the yellow gap columns (38-39) which correspond to the black bar's gap.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 131 actions; a strong score needs about 7 or fewer. You are 18.7x over the target.
NET-ZERO WASTE: your last 51 actions returned the board to a state already seen 51 actions ago — that round-trip made no progress yet still cost 51 actions. Do not extend-and-retract or oscillate; a sequence that ends where it began is pure waste.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the ascii:
Row 0: PPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPPccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccccc
Row 1: GGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGGWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWWW
So the bar is at row 0, with pink (P) on the left and charcoal (c) on the right.
Row 1 has dark gray (G) on the left and white (W) on the right.
Current state: c_bar=37, P=27.
I'm completely stuck. I've tried almost everything. Let me think about this from a completely different angle.
Wait, I just noticed something. The bar is at row 0, and it has pink (P) on the left and charcoal (c) on the right. The charcoal section grows from the right, not from the left. This is consistent with what I've observed.
But what if the goal is NOT to fill the bar, but to EMPTY the bar? For example, what if clicking on the bar at certain positions removes charcoal pixels?
Actually, I've observed that clicking on the bar always adds charcoal pixels, never removes them. So this probably won't help.
Hmm, let me think about this differently. What if the goal is to click on the bar at positions that correspond to the OBJECTS' horizontal positions, but in a specific order? For example:
1. Click on bar at col 20 (black bar left)
2. Click on bar at col 38 (yellow gap)
3. Click on bar at col 40 (black bar right)
4. Click on bar at col 48 (charcoal object)
5. Click on bar at col 50 (yellow strip)
6. Click on bar at col 60 (blue block 1)
7. Click on bar at col 60 (blue block 2)
But I've clicked on the bar at various positions and they all just add +1. So this probably won't help.
OK, I'm going to try one more thing. What if the goal is to click on the objects in the order of their VERTICAL positions (top to bottom)? The objects are:
1. Blue block 1: row 24 (id=4)
2. Black bar left: row 28 (id=5)
3. Yellow gap: row 28 (id=6)
4. Black bar right: row 28 (id=7)
5. Blue block 2: row 32 (id=9)
6. Charcoal object: row 44 (id=10)
7. Yellow strip: row 44 (id=11)
So the order by vertical position is: 4, 5, 6, 7, 9, 10, 11. This is the same as the ID order I tried before!
Hmm, what if the order is by HORIZONTAL position (left to right)? The objects are:
1. Black bar left: col 20 (id=5)
2. Yellow gap: col 38 (id=6)
3. Black bar right: col 40 (id=7)
4. Charcoal object: col 48 (id=10)
5. Yellow strip: col 50 (id=11)
6. Blue block 1: col 60 (id=4)
7. Blue block 2: col 60 (id=9)
So the order by horizontal position is: 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 4, 9. Let me try this order!
But wait, I need to reset the bar first. The bar is currently at c_bar=37, P=27. If I click on 7 objects, the bar will go to c_bar=37+9=46 (assuming the charcoal object adds +2 and the rest add +1 each). This won't trigger a game over (which happens at c_bar=64).
Actually, let me just try the 7-object sequence in horizontal order: 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 4, 9.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: c_bar=37, P=27. Bar is at row 0, pink on left, charcoal on right. All previous hypotheses failed after 131 actions.
Plan: Try clicking on the 7 foreground objects in LEFT-TO-RIGHT order (by horizontal position): 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 4, 9. This is a new order I haven't tried.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Get boundaries for the 7 foreground objects in LEFT-TO-RIGHT order
seg = current_frame.segmentation
# Order by leftmost column: 5 (col 20), 6 (col 38), 7 (col 40), 10 (col 48), 11 (col 50), 4 (col 60), 9 (col 60)
targets = [5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 4, 9]
actions = []
for tid in targets:
    n = next(node for node in seg['nodes'] if node['id'] == tid)
    row, col = n['boundary'][0]
    actions.append({'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': row, 'col': col})
    print(f"Click id={tid} ({n['color']}, {n['pixels']}px) at ({row}, {col})")

action(actions)

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ('P', 'c'):
        print(f"  {n['color']}: {n['pixels']} pixels")
print(f"Level completed: {last_action_result.get('level_completed', False)}")
print(f"Done: {last_action_result.get('done', False)}")
print(f"Game over: {last_action_result.get('game_over', False)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:

Click id=6 (Y, 8px) at (28, 38)
Click id=7 (B, 96px) at (28, 40)
Click id=10 (c, 16px) at (44, 48)
Click id=11 (Y, 12px) at (44, 50)
Click id=4 (b, 16px) at (24, 60)
Click id=9 (b, 16px) at (32, 60)
  P: 18 pixels
  c: 46 pixels
  c: 16 pixels
Level completed: False
Done: False
Game over: False
